mission.title: Install and Configure Redis Operator (Spotahome / OT-CONTAINER-KIT)
mission.description: The Redis Operator by OT-CONTAINER-KIT simplifies the management of Redis instances on Kubernetes, supporting standalone, cluster, and sentinel modes. It provides built-in monitoring and adheres to best practices for security and performance. Ideal for organizations looking to automate Redis deployments and management in a Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Redis Operator Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add redis-operator https://ot-container-kit.github.io/redis-operator

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the Redis Operator Helm repo.
> Updates the Helm repository to fetch the latest charts.

steps.title: Install Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
CRDs are necessary for the Redis Operator to function correctly, allowing it to manage Redis resources.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OT-CONTAINER-KIT/redis-operator/v0.24.0/deploy/crds/redis.opstree.dev_redisclusters_crd.yaml

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OT-CONTAINER-KIT/redis-operator/v0.24.0/deploy/crds/redis.opstree.dev_redisfailovers_crd.yaml

```

> Installs the RedisCluster CRD.
> Installs the RedisFailover CRD.

steps.title: Install Redis Operator
steps.description:
This command installs the Redis Operator in the specified namespace, allowing it to manage Redis instances.

```bash

helm install redis-operator redis-operator/redis-operator --namespace redis-operator --create-namespace --version 0.24.0

```

> Installs the Redis Operator with the specified version.

steps.title: Create a Redis Cluster
steps.description:
This step creates a Redis cluster using a custom resource definition, enabling the operator to manage it.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: redis.opstree.dev/v1
kind: RedisCluster
metadata:
  name: my-redis-cluster
  namespace: redis-operator
spec:
  size: 3
  redis:
    image: redis:7.0.0
EOF

```

> Creates a Redis cluster named 'my-redis-cluster' with 3 replicas.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: redis.opstree.dev/v1
kind: RedisCluster
metadata:
  name: my-redis-cluster
  namespace: redis-operator
spec:
  size: 3
  redis:
    image: redis:7.0.0
EOF

steps.title: Verify Redis Cluster
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the Redis cluster to ensure it is running as expected.

```bash

kubectl get redisclusters -n redis-operator

```

> Verifies the Redis cluster status.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Redis Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Redis Operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this may lead to data loss if persistent volumes are not retained.

```bash

helm uninstall redis-operator --namespace redis-operator

kubectl delete namespace redis-operator

```

> Uninstalls the Redis Operator.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Redis Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your Redis data. After upgrading, verify that the operator is functioning correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n redis-operator -o name | xargs -I {} kubectl drain {} --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

helm upgrade redis-operator redis-operator/redis-operator --namespace redis-operator --version 0.24.0

kubectl get pods -n redis-operator

```

> Drains the Redis operator pods.
> Upgrades the Redis Operator to the specified version.
> Verifies that the Redis Operator pods are running.
commands: kubectl get pods -n redis-operator -o name | xargs -I {} kubectl drain {} --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create RedisCluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the CRD is not installed or if there are validation errors in the CRD.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the CRDs are installed correctly and validate the RedisCluster YAML configuration.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Redis pod is CrashLoopBackOff'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This typically indicates an issue with the Redis configuration or resource limits.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Redis pod using 'kubectl logs <pod-name>' and adjust the configuration or resource limits accordingly.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'No resources found in redis-operator namespace'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Redis Operator has not been installed correctly or the namespace is incorrect.

**Fix:**
Verify the installation steps and ensure that the correct namespace is being used.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'RedisCluster is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may happen if the Redis pods are not running or are in a failed state.

**Fix:**
Check the status of the Redis pods with 'kubectl get pods -n redis-operator' and troubleshoot any issues.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Redis 7.0.0, improved monitoring capabilities with redis-exporter.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Redis cluster listed with the command 'kubectl get redisclusters -n redis-operator', and the status should be 'Ready'.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: redis.opstree.dev/v1
kind: RedisCluster
metadata:
  name: my-redis-cluster
  namespace: redis-operator
spec:
  size: 3
  redis:
    image: redis:7.0.0
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the required tools installed.
